description:
Background:
    You are acting as a penetration tester who has already run an Nmap scan against a local target.  
    The raw scan output has been saved for you at

        /home/user/network_targets/sample_nmap_output.txt

    Your job now is to transform that human-readable Nmap output into a machine-readable CSV report that lists **only** the open TCP ports.

    Required actions (2-3 shell commands in total are enough):
    1. Parse the file /home/user/network_targets/sample_nmap_output.txt, filter every line that represents an open TCP port, and extract the three data fields “Port number”, “State”, and “Service”.  
       • A line that represents an open TCP port matches the pattern “\<number\>/tcp  open  \<service\>”.  
       • Drop “/tcp” and retain just the integer port number in the first column.  
    2. Sort the resulting list numerically by port number in ascending order.  
    3. Write the result to the file

        /home/user/scan_reports/open_ports.csv

       The directory /home/user/scan_reports already exists and is writable by your user account.

    CSV format that the automated checker will verify:
        • First line must be the exact header:
              Port,State,Service
        • Each subsequent line must follow the exact pattern:
              <integer>,open,<service-name>
        • Columns are separated by a single comma, no spaces before or after the comma.
        • End the file with a single trailing newline (UNIX line ending).

    Example of the **exact** structure the checker expects (the port numbers and services correspond to the sample input file, so do not change their order once sorted):

        Port,State,Service
        22,open,ssh
        80,open,http
        111,open,rpcbind
        443,open,https
        3306,open,mysql

    After creating /home/user/scan_reports/open_ports.csv, print its contents to STDOUT so the reviewer can see the result immediately.

    Note:
    • You do NOT need to run Nmap; the scan data is already present.  
    • Use any standard command-line utilities available by default in most Linux distributions (e.g., grep, awk, cut, sed, sort, etc.).  
    • The entire task can be completed with just a short pipeline of 2-3 commands separated by pipes and a redirect.  
    • Do not add any extra columns or rows.  
    • Do not delete or rename the sample_nmap_output.txt file.

    Deliverables that will be automatically checked:
        1. The file /home/user/scan_reports/open_ports.csv exists.
        2. Its contents exactly match the required CSV structure described above.
        3. You echoed that same content to STDOUT at the end of your session.
truth:
Pre-existing filesystem entities:

    1. Directory: /home/user/network_targets
       Permissions: 755 (owner=user)
    2. File: /home/user/network_targets/sample_nmap_output.txt
       Permissions: 644 (owner=user)
       Exact content:

Starting Nmap 7.93 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2023-06-15 12:34 UTC
Nmap scan report for localhost (127.0.0.1)
Host is up (0.000093s latency).
Not shown: 995 closed tcp ports (reset)
PORT     STATE SERVICE
22/tcp   open  ssh
25/tcp   filtered smtp
80/tcp   open  http
111/tcp  open  rpcbind
139/tcp  closed netbios-ssn
443/tcp  open  https
3306/tcp open  mysql

Nmap done: 1 IP address (1 host up) scanned in 0.19 seconds


    Directories that must be created by the provisioning script (and already exist before the agent starts):

    3. Directory: /home/user/scan_reports
       Permissions: 755 (owner=user)

    Target file expected after successful completion by the agent:

    4. File: /home/user/scan_reports/open_ports.csv
       Expected exact content (with a final trailing newline, \n):

Port,State,Service
22,open,ssh
80,open,http
111,open,rpcbind
443,open,https
3306,open,mysql
